hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_desc: Delegate and empower your team using causative have and get -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Developing Your Team
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 18 -- Developing Your Team

listenings.text: When I was first promoted, I did what most new managers do. I kept doing the work myself, only faster, because I was good at it. And for a while it looked like it was working. But my team wasn't growing, and I was exhausted. The change came when I stopped doing and started developing. Now, instead of leading every negotiation, I have my best people run them. I get them to make the real decisions, not just carry my bag. I give them ownership, and yes, I stay hands-off, even when it's uncomfortable. Of course they make mistakes. Last month I had a junior lead a supplier call, and it didn't go perfectly. So we debriefed, and this week she's leading it again. People think that's a failure of delegation. It isn't. That is delegation working. The strongest teams are built by leaders who are brave enough to get the work done through others, and patient enough to let them learn.

Welcome to this short segment on the causative, the grammar of getting things done through other people. There are two situations to keep apart. First, when a person does something for you. Use have plus the person plus the base verb, with no to. I had her present the results. Or use get plus the person plus to plus the verb. I got him to take ownership. Same idea, but get needs the little word to, and have does not. Second, when a thing is done for you, and it doesn't matter who does it. Use have or get plus the object plus the past participle. I had the report reviewed. I got the slides redesigned. The trick is to notice whether you are talking about a person doing something or a thing being done, and then choose the right form. Master this, and you can talk about delegation like a leader.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
to give a task or responsibility to someone else
b
to give someone the authority and confidence to act
c
to guide and advise a less experienced colleague
d
the feeling of being responsible for a task or result
e
ability that has not yet been fully developed
f
to care for someone and help them grow
g
giving people freedom to work without close control
h
to take on more responsibility when it is needed
i
being responsible for results and answerable for them
j
to control every small detail of someone's work
k
the freedom to make your own decisions
l
how quickly someone must learn something new

paras: There is a moment in every manager's career when doing more stops working. You are good at the job -- that is why you were promoted -- so your instinct is to keep doing it, only faster and for longer. But a team does not grow around a leader who does everything. It grows around a leader who does less, and develops the people who do the rest. The shift from doing to developing is the hardest one most managers ever make, because it means letting go of the very work that made you successful.

The tool for that shift is delegation, and delegation is not dumping. When you delegate well, you give a person real ownership, not just a task -- you have them run the process, you get them to make the decisions, and you stay hands-off enough that they feel the weight of it. You will be tempted to micromanage, to hover, to fix every small thing. Resist. Autonomy is uncomfortable, but it is the only thing that turns potential into skill. The people you nurture with real responsibility are the ones who step up when it matters.

None of this means abandoning them. A good coach mentors from beside, not above: available for the hard questions, clear about accountability, and honest that there will be a learning curve. You had the junior lead the call; it did not go perfectly; you debrief and go again. That is not a failure of delegation -- it is delegation working. The leaders who build the strongest teams are simply the ones who were brave enough to get the work done through others, and patient enough to let those others learn.
reading.source: Adapted from a leadership development guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A good leader grows a team by doing all the important work personally and quickly.
Teams grow when a leader shifts from doing to developing -- delegating real ownership, staying hands-off, and coaching through the learning curve.
Delegation means giving people tasks but keeping all the decisions and control yourself.

items: The text says the shift from doing to developing is easy for most managers.
It calls it the hardest shift most managers ever make, because it means letting go of the work that made them successful.
According to the text, delegating well means giving real ownership, not just a task.
The second paragraph says delegation is not dumping -- you give a person real ownership, have them run the process, and stay hands-off.
The text recommends micromanaging to make sure delegation succeeds.
It says resist the temptation to micromanage; autonomy is what turns potential into skill.
The text says a call that does not go perfectly can still mean delegation is working.
The last paragraph says you debrief and go again -- that is not a failure of delegation, it is delegation working.

parts: Andrea coaches Oskar to step up. This quarter she will not lead the negotiations herself -- she will
Oskar run the whole process, to give him real
. She promises to mentor him but stay
him make a small mistake than micromanage him. To help him prepare, she
the rate sheets forwarded to him that morning.

gapfill.text: Causative forms: 'have + person + base verb' ('have Oskar run it') and 'get + person + to + verb' ('get him to make the call') delegate to a person; 'have / get + object + past participle' ('had the sheets forwarded') arranges for a thing to be done. 'have' takes no 'to'; 'get + person' does.

bank.label: Useful language for coaching and delegating
items: This time, I'm going to have you run the whole process.
I want to get you to take ownership of this.
I'll mentor you, but I'll stay hands-off.
You have the potential; you just need the reps.
I'd rather you learn than have me micromanage you.
I had the rate sheets forwarded to you this morning.
Autonomy is how you grow into the role.
Step up on this one, and I'll be right behind you.

lf.title: Read each line and complete the causative structure
items: I got the team to take

lf.text: 'Have + person + base verb' and 'get + person + to + verb' delegate a task to a person; 'have / get + object + past participle' arranges for a thing to be done. Remember: 'have' = no 'to', 'get' = 'to' before the person's verb.

items.situation: Delegate the client call to a team member.
Encourage a hesitant colleague to take the decision.
tips: 'I want to get you to decide this.'

items.situation: Arrange for a proposal to be reviewed by someone else.
A colleague fears a stretch task. Reassure and empower them.
tips: 'I'll stay hands-off, but I've got you.'

items.situation: A delegated task went wrong. Coach, don't take it back.
tips: Treat it as the learning curve.

items: Delegate a task to a team member using 'have + person + verb' (e.g. 'I'll have you lead...').
Empower someone to own a decision, using 'get + person + to + verb'.
Arrange for a document to be prepared by someone else, using 'have / get + object + past participle'.
A colleague is nervous about a stretch task. Coach them: name their potential, promise support, and stay hands-off.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = have, 2 = ownership, 3 = hands-off, 4 = get, 5 = had.
Delegate to a person: 'I'm going to have you run the process.'
Empower with get: 'I want to get you to take ownership.'
Thing done for you: 'I had the rate sheets forwarded.'
answerkey.note: have + person + base verb (no 'to'); get + person + to + verb; have / get + object + past participle for a thing done for you.
